4.8.a.info:
The SSH 6-step recipe is the canonical sequence for enabling Secure Shell on a Cisco IOS device. It is a near-guaranteed lab simulation on the CCNA 200-301 exam and must be memorized in exact order because each step depends on the previous one.

The six steps in strict order:

  • (1) hostname R1 — gives the device a non-default name. RSA key generation uses hostname.domain-name as the key label. The default 'Router' or 'Switch' may cause key generation to fail.
  • (2) ip domain-name example.com — required so the device can construct a fully qualified domain name (FQDN) for the RSA key.
  • (3) crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 — generates the asymmetric key pair used for SSH session encryption. 2048 is the recommended modulus.
  • (4) ip ssh version 2 — forces SSHv2 only. Without this, the device runs the hybrid '1.99' which still accepts insecure SSHv1.
  • (5) username admin privilege 15 secret MySecret — creates the local user database entry. secret (hashed) is mandatory, not password (plaintext).
  • (6) line vty 0 15 → transport input ssh → login local — exposes SSH on the 16 virtual terminal lines and ties them to the local user database.

Why the order matters: Steps 1 and 2 are prerequisites for step 3 (RSA gen needs the FQDN). Steps 3 and 4 are prerequisites for step 6 (VTY SSH transport needs a key and a version). Step 5 is a prerequisite for login local in step 6. Reordering breaks the chain — the exam sim will fail silently if you skip ahead.

Verification commands after the recipe: show ip ssh (server state, version, timeout, retries), show ssh (active sessions), and a test SSH connection from another device: ssh -l admin 10.1.1.1.

hack.memory: HD-RVUL: Hostname, Domain, RSA, Version, User, Line. Chant it: 'Hot Dogs Really Very Underrated Lately.' Each letter = one step. Forget one = SSH dies. Muscle-memory the whole sequence — type it in Packet Tracer 5 times in a row until your fingers remember it without thinking.
hack.practice: Open Packet Tracer, drop a 2911 router, and run the full 6-step recipe from scratch three times. After each run, wipe with 'write erase' + reload and do it again. Time yourself — target under 90 seconds. When you can execute the recipe blindfolded, you are exam-ready for this specific sim.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 walks through every step with a lab. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 (Configuring IPv4 Addresses and Static Routes and Device Management) has the full recipe. CCNA exam places at least one SSH sim — and missing any step equals full-credit loss on the question.

4.8.a.info:
Every step in the SSH recipe is mandatory. Skipping even one produces a silent or misleading failure that wastes lab-sim time on the exam. Knowing which step maps to which failure mode is the difference between fixing the sim in 30 seconds and running out the clock.

Failure map — step skipped → observed symptom:

  • Skip hostname (1): RSA key generates with label Router.domain. Some IOS versions refuse to generate; others generate but SSH behaves inconsistently.
  • Skip domain-name (2): crypto key generate rsa returns '% Please define a domain-name first.' Complete blocker.
  • Skip RSA key (3): show ip ssh reports 'SSH Disabled - version 1.99.' No encryption = no SSH.
  • Skip ip ssh version 2 (4): Device falls back to hybrid 1.99, still accepts insecure SSHv1. Security weakness — often tested conceptually.
  • Skip username secret (5): login local has no database to check. All SSH logins get 'Password required, but none set.'
  • Skip line vty config (6): Default VTY may accept Telnet only (or nothing). SSH client sees 'Connection refused.'

Debug sequence when SSH fails: Run show running-config | include hostname|domain|ssh|username|vty and compare each line against the 6-step checklist. The missing line is the missing step.

Exam mindset: The sim grader checks specific running-config lines. A missed line = a missed point. Treat the recipe as a binary checklist — all green or you lose the sim.

hack.memory: Each step is a link in a chain. Break one link = whole chain broken. Think 'all-or-nothing.' When SSH fails on the exam sim, run 'show run | i hostname|domain|ssh|username|vty' — the missing line IS the bug. Fix that one line and move on.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure SSH but deliberately skip step 3 (no RSA key). Then try to SSH in. Read the error. Wipe, repeat skipping step 5 (no username). Read the error. Do this for all 6 steps. Memorize which failure matches which missed step — this is invaluable on the exam sim.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The CCNA sim auto-grader checks for specific lines in running-config. Partial credit is rare — either all 6 steps are present or you lose the question. Debugging speed matters when the sim timer is ticking.

4.8.b.info:
The hostname and ip domain-name commands together form the fully qualified domain name (FQDN) that the RSA key generator needs to label the key pair. Both must be configured before running crypto key generate rsa — otherwise key generation fails or produces an unusable key.

How the FQDN is built: If hostname R1 and ip domain-name example.com are configured, the RSA key is labeled R1.example.com. You can verify this with show crypto key mypubkey rsa — the 'Key name' field shows the FQDN.

Commands:

Router(config)# hostname R1
R1(config)# ip domain-name example.com
R1(config)# crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048

Common failure signature: Running crypto key generate rsa with no domain-name returns % Please define a domain-name first. This is the most common SSH lab error because students jump to the RSA step.

Why Cisco built it this way: Certificate and key infrastructure on enterprise networks rely on FQDNs (R1.example.com) rather than bare names (R1). Using the FQDN for the key label makes keys globally unique within the administrative domain, avoiding collisions when keys are exported, shared, or used in certificate requests.

No DNS server is required. The domain name is a local string used only for labeling — the router does not need to resolve it. Any valid-looking string works: lab.local, ccna.study, example.com.

hack.memory: Hostname = first name. Domain-name = last name. Together = full name on the RSA key's birth certificate. You cannot be born without a full name — RSA refuses. Mnemonic: 'No FQDN, no RSA.' First step every SSH lab: set hostname AND domain-name before touching crypto.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, try running 'crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048' with ONLY a hostname set — watch it fail with '% Please define a domain-name first.' Now add 'ip domain-name lab.local' and retry. Key generates. Run 'show crypto key mypubkey rsa' to see the key name 'R1.lab.local' confirmed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam often embeds this as a troubleshooting question: 'The administrator runs crypto key generate rsa and receives an error. What command is missing?' Answer: ip domain-name.

4.8.b.info:
If the hostname is left as the default Router or Switch, the RSA key will still generate on most IOS versions, but with the generic label Router.example.com — and on some IOS trains the generator refuses outright with an error. Either way, leaving the default hostname is a red flag in a production (or exam) configuration.

Why the default is a problem:

  • Key label collisions: Every out-of-the-box Cisco device has hostname 'Router.' If two untouched routers are joined in the same administrative domain, both RSA keys have the same label — a collision that breaks key-based workflows.
  • Operator confusion: When you SSH in, the prompt reads Router# — giving no clue which device you're on. In a lab with 5 routers all named 'Router,' this is a disaster.
  • Exam grading: CCNA lab sims check for a renamed device. Leaving the default hostname loses points even if SSH technically works.

Correct sequence: Always rename first:

Router(config)# hostname R1
R1(config)# ip domain-name example.com
R1(config)# crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048

What if you already generated the key with the default hostname? Delete and regenerate:

R1(config)# crypto key zeroize rsa
R1(config)# crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048

The new key picks up the new hostname automatically. Any existing SSH sessions survive until they disconnect; future connections use the new key.

hack.memory: Default 'Router' hostname = generic ID badge. Change it first, every time. Mnemonic: 'Rename before RSA.' If you generated the key with the default and need to fix it: 'crypto key zeroize rsa' wipes the key, then regenerate after renaming. Simple and non-destructive to config.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, leave hostname as 'Router' and run the full SSH recipe. Then SSH in — prompt reads 'Router#.' Now 'crypto key zeroize rsa', 'hostname R1', regenerate key. SSH back in — prompt reads 'R1#.' This makes the hostname-vs-key relationship click.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam tests this as a 'why did SSH fail?' question — answer: hostname was still 'Router' or there was no hostname set before key generation.

4.8.c.info:
crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 creates the RSA public/private key pair that powers SSH's encryption and server authentication. This is step 3 of the 6-step recipe and the step that actually enables SSH — before this, show ip ssh reports 'SSH Disabled.'

What the modulus means: The modulus is the bit length of the RSA key. Larger = stronger but slower. Cisco IOS supports 360-4096 bits. The exam answer is 2048 — it is the modern minimum for production and what all Cisco documentation recommends.

  • 768 — legacy minimum for SSHv2 enablement. Too weak for real use.
  • 1024 — deprecated. Attackable with modern compute.
  • 2048 — exam and production answer. Good balance of security and performance.
  • 4096 — stronger but noticeably slower on low-end IOS devices. Overkill for CCNA.

Interactive vs one-liner form: If you type crypto key generate rsa without a modulus, IOS prompts interactively: 'How many bits in the modulus [512]:'. The one-liner crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 is non-interactive and safer to script.

Key label: Defaults to hostname.domain-name. You can also use named keys with label [name] and modulus [size] parameters, but for CCNA stick with the default.

Regenerating: To replace an existing key, zeroize first:

R1(config)# crypto key zeroize rsa
R1(config)# crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048

Verification: show crypto key mypubkey rsa shows the key label, usage (General Purpose / Signing / Encryption), and key data. Absence of output = no key generated.

items: Verify: show crypto key mypubkey rsa

hack.memory: 2048 = the CCNA answer, every time. Mnemonic: 'Two thousand forty-eight keeps the hackers away.' If the exam offers 1024 and 2048 — pick 2048. If offered 2048 and 4096 — still pick 2048 (Cisco default recommendation). The number to memorize: 2-0-4-8.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: 'crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048'. Verify with 'show crypto key mypubkey rsa' — note the label and modulus. Zeroize with 'crypto key zeroize rsa' and regenerate with modulus 1024. Compare 'show ip ssh' output before and after — notice the version and enabled state change.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA frequently tests: 'Which modulus is recommended for SSHv2?' — answer 2048. Also tests the command syntax verbatim; spelling errors in lab sim lose points.

4.8.c.info:
768 bits is the absolute minimum modulus size required to enable SSHv2 on Cisco IOS. If you generate a smaller key (e.g., 512), IOS will only run SSHv1 — which is insecure and should never be used.

Key size thresholds:

  • < 768 bits: SSHv2 cannot be enabled. Only SSHv1 is possible.
  • = 768 bits: The minimum bar for SSHv2. Still considered weak.
  • ≥ 1024 bits: Working SSHv2 level but deprecated by modern crypto guidance.
  • ≥ 2048 bits: The recommended minimum for real deployments and the CCNA exam.

Exam distinction: The exam sometimes asks 'What is the minimum modulus for SSHv2?' (answer: 768) and separately asks 'What modulus should you configure?' (answer: 2048). These are two different questions with two different answers — do not conflate them.

Why 768 matters historically: Early IOS required at least 768 bits because RSA keys below that could not safely accommodate SSHv2's larger message payloads and key exchange structures. Modern IOS still respects this floor — it is hard-coded into the SSHv2 state machine.

Practical rule: Never choose 768 or 1024 in a real configuration. The only reason to know 768 is to answer the trivia question on the exam.

hack.memory: Two numbers, two questions. 'Minimum for SSHv2?' = 768. 'What should I configure?' = 2048. Never mix them up. Trick: 768 is the floor, 2048 is the target. 'Set the floor at 768, live at 2048.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer try generating a 512-bit key and see if SSHv2 can be enabled — it cannot. Then generate a 768-bit key — SSHv2 can be enabled but is weak. Then regenerate at 2048 for production. This exercise reinforces the two-number split.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 mentions the 768 threshold briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam occasionally offers both 768 and 2048 as choices; picking correctly requires knowing which question is being asked — minimum vs recommended.

4.8.d.info:
ip ssh version 2 is a global configuration command that forces the SSH server to accept only SSHv2 connections. Without this command, the device runs in '1.99' compatibility mode — which accepts both insecure SSHv1 and secure SSHv2 clients. Running compatibility mode is a security weakness because an attacker can force a protocol downgrade.

Command:

R1(config)# ip ssh version 2

What changes: Before the command, show ip ssh shows 'SSH Enabled - version 1.99.' After the command, it shows 'SSH Enabled - version 2.0.' The '1.99' is not a typo — it is the RFC 4253 identifier for a server that supports both v1 and v2.

Why v1 is forbidden:

  • Integrity attacks: SSHv1 uses CRC-32 for integrity, which is vulnerable to targeted bit-flip attacks.
  • Weaker key exchange: SSHv1 uses a simpler RSA-based exchange without Diffie-Hellman forward secrecy.
  • Single MAC: SSHv1 supports only one message authentication algorithm; SSHv2 negotiates from a suite.
  • Deprecated: IETF deprecated SSHv1 in 2006. Modern clients (OpenSSH 7.6+) removed SSHv1 support entirely.

Verification: show ip ssh reports the version. Also, attempting SSHv1 from a compatible client will be rejected after this command is applied.

hack.memory: 'ip ssh version 2' = lock the door against v1 attackers. Without it, device advertises '1.99' = 'I accept v1 AND v2' — a weakness. With it, device says '2.0 only.' Type this command every time after crypto key generation. No exceptions.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: complete steps 1-3, skip step 4, then run 'show ip ssh.' Note version 1.99. Add 'ip ssh version 2.' Run 'show ip ssh' again. Version changes to 2.0. Practice this toggle until you can spot the 1.99/2.0 difference instantly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam tests this as both a fill-in sim (type 'ip ssh version 2') and a conceptual question ('Which command forces SSHv2 only?').

4.8.d.info:
Enforcing SSHv2 is a security hardening step, not a functional one — SSHv1 would technically work, but it exposes the device to well-known protocol-level attacks. Modern compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, CIS Cisco Benchmarks) explicitly require SSHv2-only.

SSHv1 specific vulnerabilities:

  • Insertion attacks (CVE-2001-0572): Allows a man-in-the-middle to inject arbitrary data into the stream.
  • Weak integrity (CRC-32 compensation attack): An attacker can modify ciphertext and predictably alter plaintext on decryption.
  • No forward secrecy: If the RSA key is compromised later, all past sessions can be decrypted retroactively.
  • Single session key: The entire session uses one key; rotation mid-session is not supported.

SSHv2 improvements:

  • Diffie-Hellman key exchange with forward secrecy (ephemeral keys per session).
  • Strong MAC algorithms (HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA512).
  • Separate encryption and integrity keys.
  • Negotiable cipher suites for future upgrades.
  • Support for public-key authentication in addition to passwords.

Exam mindset: You will not configure ciphers or MACs on CCNA. You just need to remember: 'v1 is broken, v2 is mandatory.' Any question asking 'why v2 instead of v1?' answers 'stronger encryption and integrity.'

'1.99' compatibility mode trap: The exam loves this. If a config has no ip ssh version 2, the device runs 1.99 and accepts v1. The fix is always 'add ip ssh version 2' — not 'upgrade the IOS,' not 'regenerate the key.'

hack.memory: v1 = broken crypto from the 1990s. v2 = modern crypto. If an exam question asks 'why v2?' — the answer is always 'stronger encryption and key exchange.' If it asks about '1.99' — that is the dangerous hybrid mode. Kill it with 'ip ssh version 2.'
hack.practice: Read the RFC 4253 abstract (5 minutes) to see how SSHv2 is structured differently from v1. Even a skim helps. Then in Packet Tracer, inspect 'show ip ssh' output — note the version field and understand '1.99' means 'I still accept v1, the dangerous one.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 briefly mentions why v2 matters. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA 200-301 tests this as a recognition question — you need to be able to say 'SSHv1 is insecure, v2 is required' on command.

4.8.e.info:
username admin secret MySecret creates a local user account in the router's running configuration. The secret keyword stores the password as a one-way hash (type 5 MD5 on older IOS, type 9 scrypt on newer IOS, type 8 PBKDF2-SHA256 in between). This is the only correct way to create a local user for SSH on the CCNA.

Full syntax variations:

  • username admin secret MySecret — basic local user with hashed password.
  • username admin privilege 15 secret MySecret — user starts at privilege level 15 (full enable) on login. No separate enable command needed.
  • username admin algorithm-type scrypt secret MySecret — force type 9 scrypt hashing (strongest).

What appears in running-config:

username admin privilege 15 secret 9 $9$Nb8iEj7Yz2U...

The '9' is the hash type. The string after is the salted hash. It is not reversible — not even Cisco can recover the original password from this hash.

Why privilege 15? Cisco IOS privilege levels run 0-15. Level 1 is EXEC mode (limited). Level 15 is privileged EXEC (everything). Setting privilege 15 in the username command drops the user straight into R1# on login. Without it, the user lands in R1> and must type enable — which requires a separate enable secret.

Hook to step 6: The local user database is useless without login local on the VTY lines. That command tells VTY authentication to look up the local database. Without both halves, SSH has no way to authenticate.

hack.memory: 'secret' not 'password' — every time. Mnemonic: 'Shh! secret!' One-way hash that even Cisco cannot reverse. Add 'privilege 15' so the user drops straight into enable mode on SSH login. Full command: 'username admin privilege 15 secret MySecret.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: create 'username admin privilege 15 secret MyPass123.' Run 'show run | include username' — observe the '$9$...' hash. Try to read the password from it (you cannot). Now create 'username baduser password BadPass' — observe the plaintext in the config. Delete baduser. This contrast cements 'secret' as mandatory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 and Day 4 (device management). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA sims check for the 'secret' keyword specifically. Using 'password' loses the point even if the rest is correct.

4.8.e.info:
The difference between secret and password in the username command is a security-critical exam trap. They look similar but produce dramatically different results in the running-config.

secret keyword:

  • Stores password as a one-way hash (type 5 MD5, type 8 PBKDF2, or type 9 scrypt).
  • Cannot be reversed — even with the running-config, the original password is not recoverable.
  • Always use this for CCNA, production, and any security-conscious config.

password keyword:

  • Stores password as type 0 (plaintext) by default.
  • With service password-encryption enabled, becomes type 7 — Cisco's proprietary obfuscation, trivially reversible with any online decoder.
  • Never use this for CCNA exam lab sims or production.

Side-by-side in running-config:

username alice secret 9 $9$Nb8iEj7Yz2U...(unreadable)
username bob password 0 MyPlainPass123       (visible)
username carol password 7 070C285F4D06       (trivially decoded)

Type 7 is not encryption. The Cisco 'type 7' scheme is a simple XOR cipher with a known key. Any online 'Cisco type 7 decoder' reveals the password in milliseconds. Treat type 7 as plaintext.

Exam mindset: Any config on the exam that uses username ... password ... is either a troubleshooting target ('what is wrong?') or a trap in a multiple choice. The right answer is always secret.

hack.memory: secret = Secure (hash). password = Plaintext (or trivially reversed). 'S for Strong, P for Problem.' On the exam, if a config has 'username X password Y' — flag it as insecure. The fix is always to change 'password' to 'secret.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure both 'username alice secret Hello' and 'username bob password Hello' with 'service password-encryption' on. Copy the running-config into a text file. Find 'alice' (hash is opaque). Find 'bob' (type 7 string). Paste bob's string into an online type-7 decoder — you'll see 'Hello.' This makes the vulnerability visceral.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 and Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA 200-301 regularly tests secret-vs-password. Know that type 7 is not secure — it is just obfuscation. Only type 5/8/9 (via secret) is true hashing.

4.8.f.info:
line vty 0 15 enters configuration mode for all 16 Virtual Teletype (VTY) lines simultaneously. VTY lines are the inbound telnet/SSH sessions — each concurrent remote login uses one VTY line. A command issued under line vty 0 15 applies uniformly to all 16 lines.

Why 0 through 15? Older IOS versions had only 5 VTY lines (0-4). Modern IOS increased to 16 lines (0-15) to support more concurrent administrative sessions. Configuring all 16 is best practice — if you configure only 0-4 and a 6th admin tries to SSH in, they hit line 5 with default (unsecured) config and either get a silent rejection or worse, a fallback login path.

Standard block of commands under line vty:

R1(config)# line vty 0 15
R1(config-line)# transport input ssh
R1(config-line)# login local
R1(config-line)# exec-timeout 5 0
R1(config-line)# logging synchronous

Order matters for exam sims: Always configure transport and login on the full 0-15 range. Some exam sims only check lines 0-4, but some check 5-15 too — covering all 16 is guaranteed safe.

Per-line override: You can also enter a single line's config with line vty 5, but the 0-15 form is preferred for consistency.

Verification: show running-config | section line vty shows the applied config for each line block. show users shows which VTY lines are currently occupied by which users.

hack.memory: 'line vty 0 15' = sixteen inbound remote-login slots. Always configure ALL 16. Older IOS had 0-4, modern IOS has 0-15. Mnemonic: 'Sixteen seats at the SSH bar — make every seat safe.' Never configure just 0-4 on modern gear.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure only 'line vty 0 4' with transport and login. Run 'show run | section line vty' — note lines 5-15 are default (unsecured). Extend to 'line vty 0 15' and reconfigure. Now all 16 lines match. This exercise shows why the 0-15 range matters.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA sims have been known to check both the 0-4 and 5-15 ranges separately. 'line vty 0 15' is the safe, exam-correct answer.

4.8.f.info:
transport input ssh under a line vty block restricts inbound remote-access connections to SSH only. Any Telnet connection attempt is refused with 'Connection refused' or 'Connection closed by remote host.'

Variations of transport input:

  • transport input none — blocks all remote access (console only).
  • transport input telnet — Telnet only (never use).
  • transport input ssh — SSH only — the exam answer.
  • transport input telnet ssh or transport input all — both Telnet and SSH. Insecure and not recommended.

Why not 'transport input all'? Default behavior on many IOS versions is transport input all (or none in some trains). 'all' includes Telnet, which transmits everything — including passwords — in cleartext. A packet capture of a Telnet session reveals everything the admin typed, character by character.

Testing after config:

Remote# ssh -l admin 10.1.1.1      (should succeed)
Remote# telnet 10.1.1.1             (should be refused)

Pairing with ACLs: Production configurations also restrict WHICH source IPs can SSH in, using a VTY ACL: access-class 10 in under the line vty block. This is discussed in 4.8.g and 4.8.h context. For the 6-step recipe, transport input ssh is the bare minimum security.

Exam mindset: If a running-config shows transport input all or transport input telnet, the exam is asking you to fix it with transport input ssh.

hack.memory: 'transport input ssh' = SSH only, no Telnet ever. Think of it as locking the Telnet door permanently. If a config has 'transport input all' or 'transport input telnet ssh' — it is broken and needs fixing to 'transport input ssh.' One-liner fix, one-liner answer.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: complete SSH setup with 'transport input telnet ssh.' SSH in from a PC — works. Telnet in — also works. Open Wireshark on the Telnet session and watch the password appear in cleartext. Now change to 'transport input ssh.' SSH still works; Telnet refused. The security difference is dramatic and visible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA exam directly asks: 'Which command blocks Telnet and allows SSH on VTY lines?' Answer: 'transport input ssh.' Guaranteed question type.

4.8.f.info:
login local under a line vty block tells the device to authenticate inbound SSH/Telnet sessions against the local username database. Without it, SSH connections either reject the user with 'password required but none set' or fall back to line-password mode.

Three login modes on VTY:

  • login (default) — checks the password configured directly on the line (legacy, single shared password).
  • login local — checks the local username ... secret ... database. Exam answer.
  • login authentication default — delegates to AAA (TACACS+ / RADIUS). Advanced, not on CCNA sims.

How it pairs with step 5: login local only works if local usernames exist. The pairing is:

R1(config)# username admin privilege 15 secret MySecret   (Step 5)
R1(config)# line vty 0 15
R1(config-line)# login local                                (Step 6)

Common failure mode: If you configure login local but forget username ... secret ..., every SSH login attempt is rejected because the database is empty.

What the user sees:

Remote$ ssh -l admin 10.1.1.1
admin@10.1.1.1's password: [type MySecret]
R1#

With privilege 15 on the username, the login drops straight to R1#. Without it, the login lands on R1> and requires a separate enable.

Verification: show running-config | section line vty shows the login method. show users shows the authenticated username on each active VTY session.

hack.memory: 'login local' = use the local username database. Three modes: 'login' (line password, legacy), 'login local' (local usernames, exam), 'login authentication default' (AAA, advanced). Always pair with 'username X secret Y' — one without the other = SSH broken.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up SSH with 'login local' but no local usernames. Try SSH — fails. Add 'username admin secret Pass1' — SSH now works. Remove username — SSH fails again. This shows the hard dependency between step 5 and step 6.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA sims require 'login local' specifically. 'login' (legacy line password) will lose points because it requires a separate 'password' on the line and does not use the username database.

4.8.g.info:
The transport input command controls which remote-access protocols the VTY lines will accept. The difference between transport input all and transport input ssh is the difference between a secure device and a compromised one.

Full option set:

  • transport input all — accepts SSH and Telnet (and historically LAT, rlogin, v.120, etc.). Default on many IOS versions — insecure.
  • transport input none — accepts nothing. Only console access possible.
  • transport input telnet — Telnet only. Plaintext. Never use.
  • transport input ssh — SSH only. Encrypted. Exam and production answer.
  • transport input telnet ssh — both, same as 'all' for CCNA purposes. Avoid.

Attack surface comparison: With transport input all, an attacker can force a Telnet session, capture the password via packet sniffing, and log in with full credentials. With transport input ssh, all traffic is encrypted end-to-end — even if sniffed, the packets are meaningless ciphertext.

Default-check trap: Different IOS versions have different defaults. Some default to transport input none, some to transport input all. Never rely on the default — always configure transport input ssh explicitly. On the exam, if a config lacks an explicit transport input line, assume 'all' and flag it as a bug.

Hardening checklist for VTY:

  1. transport input ssh — SSH only.
  2. login local — local user auth.
  3. exec-timeout 5 0 — disconnect idle sessions.
  4. access-class 10 in — ACL to restrict source IPs.

hack.memory: 'all' = dangerous (includes Telnet). 'ssh' = safe (SSH only). If a config has 'transport input all,' treat it as a security bug on sight. Fix = 'transport input ssh.' Never leave the default — always be explicit.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set 'transport input all' on VTY 0 15. From one PC, Telnet to the router — works. From another PC, SSH to the router — also works. Change to 'transport input ssh.' Retry both — only SSH works. Verify with 'show users' that Telnet never lands a session.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. The exam contrasts 'all' vs 'ssh' directly. Know: 'all' is the vulnerable default to avoid; 'ssh' is the secure explicit answer.

4.8.g.info:
Telnet transmits every byte of the session — including usernames and passwords — in cleartext. Anyone with access to any network segment between the client and server can capture the entire session with a tool as simple as Wireshark or tcpdump, and replay the credentials to log in themselves.

What is exposed in a Telnet capture:

  • The username, typed character by character (each key press is its own packet).
  • The password, also character by character, in plaintext.
  • Every command the admin types.
  • Every byte of output the router sends back (including running-config, enable secrets displayed from show run, ACL contents, route tables).

Why this matters in real networks: Management networks are frequently 'trusted,' but that trust is often misplaced. Misconfigured switches, rogue devices, compromised jump hosts, or even a coffee-shop Wi-Fi are all places where Telnet traffic can be captured. Telnet is on the OWASP 'forbidden protocols' list and is explicitly banned by PCI DSS, HIPAA, and NIST 800-53 guidelines.

SSH by contrast: Every byte is inside an AES-encrypted tunnel. Even if the attacker captures every packet, they see only ciphertext — and without the session keys (which are ephemeral and negotiated fresh each connection), the data is unusable.

Port numbers to memorize:

  • Telnet: TCP 23 — insecure, plaintext, disable everywhere.
  • SSH: TCP 22 — secure, encrypted, the modern standard.

Exam scenarios: 'An attacker is sniffing traffic between an admin and a router. Which protocol exposes the password?' Answer: Telnet. 'Which port is used by SSH?' Answer: TCP 22. These are pure recall questions.

hack.memory: Telnet = megaphone (port 23, everyone hears). SSH = encrypted tunnel (port 22, nobody hears). Port mnemonic: 'SSH is 22, Telnet is 23 — pick the lower number, it is safer.' On the exam, Telnet is always the wrong protocol to suggest.
hack.practice: If you have access to a home lab: enable Telnet on a router, run Wireshark on the client, Telnet in with a password. Stop the capture. Follow the TCP stream — the password is there in plaintext. Now repeat with SSH — the stream is ciphertext. This one lab makes plaintext risk unforgettable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA tests plaintext risk directly: 'Why should Telnet be disabled?' Answer: transmits credentials in cleartext. Also tests Telnet port (23) vs SSH port (22) as memorization trivia.

4.8.h.info:
show ip ssh is the primary verification command for the SSH server state on a Cisco device. It answers two critical questions: 'Is SSH enabled?' and 'What version is running?' plus a handful of timer/retry parameters.

Typical output:

R1# show ip ssh
SSH Enabled - version 2.0
Authentication timeout: 120 secs; Authentication retries: 3
Minimum expected Diffie Hellman key size: 1024 bits
IOS Keys in SECSH format(ssh-rsa, base64 encoded):
ssh-rsa AAAAB3NzaC1yc2EAAAAD...

Fields to recognize on the exam:

  • SSH Enabled / Disabled — if 'Disabled,' the RSA key has not been generated (step 3 missing).
  • version 2.0 vs 1.99 — '2.0' means ip ssh version 2 is in effect. '1.99' means compatibility mode (accepts v1 + v2).
  • Authentication timeout — how long the client has to finish authenticating before the server disconnects. Default 120 seconds. Configure with ip ssh time-out [seconds].
  • Authentication retries — failed login attempts before disconnect. Default 3. Configure with ip ssh authentication-retries [count].

Common troubleshooting flow:

  1. SSH client reports 'Connection refused.' Run show ip ssh.
  2. If 'SSH Disabled' — generate RSA key (crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048).
  3. If 'version 1.99' — run ip ssh version 2.
  4. If both look correct — move on to VTY line checks (show run | section line vty).

Hardening knobs: ip ssh time-out 60 (tighter timeout), ip ssh authentication-retries 2 (fewer retries), and login block-for 600 attempts 5 within 60 (lock out brute-force attackers for 10 minutes after 5 failed logins in 60 seconds).

right.label: Quick checks
items: Timeout too long? ip ssh time-out
Too many retries? ip ssh auth-retries

hack.memory: 'show ip ssh' = 'Is the SSH server up, and how is it tuned?' Four key numbers to spot: Enabled/Disabled, version 2.0/1.99, timeout 120, retries 3. On the exam, read the output top-down and match each line to its meaning.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer after SSH setup: run 'show ip ssh.' Read each line. Then run 'ip ssh time-out 60' and 'ip ssh authentication-retries 2.' Re-run 'show ip ssh' — observe the field changes. Finally run 'crypto key zeroize rsa' and 'show ip ssh' — observe 'SSH Disabled.' Each change teaches one field.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA presents 'show ip ssh' output in multiple choice: 'What version is configured?' or 'What is the authentication timeout?' Read the field directly — no computation needed.

4.8.h.info:
show ssh (note: not show ip ssh) lists the currently active inbound SSH sessions on the device. It is the live-session monitor, whereas show ip ssh is the server-config monitor.

Typical output:

R1# show ssh
Connection Version Mode Encryption  Hmac         State          Username
0          2.0     IN   aes256-cbc  hmac-sha1    Session started  admin
0          2.0     OUT  aes256-cbc  hmac-sha1    Session started  admin

What each column means:

  • Connection — session ID (tied to a VTY line number).
  • Version — SSH protocol version in use (always 2.0 if ip ssh version 2 is set).
  • Mode — IN (traffic from client to server) and OUT (server to client). Each session shows both directions.
  • Encryption — the negotiated cipher (aes128-cbc, aes256-cbc, aes256-ctr, etc.).
  • Hmac — the message authentication algorithm (hmac-sha1, hmac-sha256, etc.).
  • State — 'Session started' means the session is fully authenticated and active.
  • Username — the authenticated user.

Companion commands:

  • show users — lists all active sessions (console + VTY) with line numbers and source IPs. Often more useful than show ssh for spotting unauthorized access.
  • clear line vty [number] — forcibly disconnects an active SSH session. Useful if someone left a session hung or you suspect a compromise.

When you run into an empty output: If show ssh returns nothing, there are no active SSH sessions. This does not mean SSH is broken — it just means nobody is currently connected. Use show ip ssh to verify the server is actually enabled.

states: User logs out OR clear line vty N

hack.memory: Two show commands, two purposes. 'show ip ssh' = server config (static). 'show ssh' = active sessions (live). Mnemonic: 'ip' is the static server IP-level config, plain 'show ssh' is the live session table. Use 'show users' as the comprehensive 'who is logged in right now?' view.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: SSH in from a PC. On the router, run 'show ssh' — see the active session. Run 'show users' — see the VTY line with source IP. From a second PC, SSH in with a second account. Run both commands again — two sessions. Then 'clear line vty 0' — watch the first session drop.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. CCNA does not heavily test 'show ssh' vs 'show ip ssh' in recent exams, but knowing the difference is valuable for troubleshooting scenarios. 'show users' is often more useful and equally tested.

4.9.a.info:
TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) is a minimalist file transfer protocol running over UDP port 69. It is deliberately stripped down: no authentication, no encryption, no directory browsing, no file metadata operations — just raw read and write of files by known filename.

Characteristics:

  • Transport: UDP 69. Connectionless. TFTP implements its own simple stop-and-wait ACK on top of UDP to handle packet loss.
  • Authentication: None. Anyone who can reach the server on UDP 69 can read or write. Server-side access control is done via file permissions or IP ACLs, not protocol auth.
  • Encryption: None. Payload travels in the clear.
  • Operations: GET (read a file) and PUT (write a file) — nothing else.
  • Block size: 512 bytes by default (negotiable up to 65,464 with RFC 2348).

Primary network-engineering use cases:

  • Backing up and restoring Cisco IOS config files (copy running-config tftp:).
  • Transferring new IOS images to flash during upgrades (copy tftp: flash:).
  • PXE boot servers for server and workstation provisioning.
  • Cisco IP phone firmware and config distribution.

When to choose TFTP: Small files, a trusted management VLAN, no authentication required, and operational simplicity matters more than security. The classic example: a flat-network lab where the admin's laptop runs TFTP server software and pushes configs to test routers.

When NOT to use TFTP: Across untrusted links, over the internet, in any environment where confidentiality or integrity matters. Use SCP instead.

hack.memory: TFTP = 'Trivially Fast, Totally Plain.' UDP 69. No auth, no crypto, no frills. Used for Cisco IOS images, config backups, and IP phone firmware — always on a trusted management VLAN. Port 69 mnemonic: '69 = the lazy protocol' (easy to remember and easy to abuse if exposed).
hack.practice: Install tftpd-hpa (Linux) or TftpD32 (Windows) on your laptop. Point a Packet Tracer router at it with 'copy running-config tftp:'. Watch the file appear in your TFTP root directory. This hands-on exercise makes the use case concrete.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9 (Device Management Protocols). CCNA exam tests TFTP port (UDP 69), auth (none), and use case (IOS backup/restore) directly. Memorize the three facts cold.

4.9.a.info:
FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is a full-featured file transfer protocol running over two TCP ports: 21 (control) and 20 (data). Unlike TFTP, FTP has proper authentication (username/password) and a rich command set (list directory, rename, delete, resume, etc.). But credentials and data both travel in cleartext — FTP is not encrypted.

Two-channel design:

  • Control channel — TCP 21: The client opens this first. Login, commands (LS, CD, GET, PUT, DEL), and responses flow here. Stays open for the entire session.
  • Data channel — TCP 20 (active mode) or negotiated port (passive mode): A separate TCP connection opens for each file transfer or directory listing, then closes when done.

Active vs Passive:

  • Active mode: Server initiates data connection from its port 20 to the client's ephemeral port. Breaks through client-side firewalls because the server is 'calling back.'
  • Passive mode (PASV): Client initiates both the control and data connections. Server listens on a high ephemeral port for data. Firewall-friendly — this is the default on modern FTP clients.

Security characteristics:

  • Authentication: Yes (username + password).
  • Encryption: No. Credentials and data are in cleartext.
  • Reliable: Yes (TCP handles retransmission).

Use cases: Large file transfers where reliability matters, user-level authentication is required, and the network is trusted. For secure alternatives: SFTP (SSH-based) or FTPS (TLS-wrapped).

hack.memory: FTP = two TCP ports (20 data + 21 control). Auth yes, encryption no. Mnemonic: 'twenty-one gives orders, twenty does the work.' Active mode = server calls back. Passive mode = client initiates both (firewall-friendly).
hack.practice: Use a local FTP server (FileZilla Server, vsftpd) and a client. Run Wireshark. Watch the USER/PASS commands appear in plaintext on port 21. Transfer a file and watch it on port 20. Now switch to passive mode and observe the negotiated ephemeral port. This lab makes FTP's two-channel design vivid.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA tests: FTP control = TCP 21, FTP data = TCP 20, auth = yes, encryption = no. Also tests active vs passive mode conceptually.

4.9.a.info:
SCP (Secure Copy Protocol) is a file transfer mechanism that runs over SSH, reusing SSH's TCP port 22, authentication, and encryption. It is the most secure file transfer option on Cisco devices and the exam answer to 'which protocol provides secure file transfer?'

How SCP works: An SSH session is established as usual (RSA key exchange, user auth, encrypted tunnel). SCP then piggybacks the file transfer inside that tunnel using the rcp-style protocol semantics. No new ports, no new authentication, no new encryption — it inherits everything from SSH.

Characteristics:

  • Transport: TCP 22 (same as SSH).
  • Authentication: Same as SSH — username/password or public-key auth.
  • Encryption: Full SSH encryption (AES, ChaCha20, etc. depending on negotiation).
  • Prerequisite on Cisco: The full 6-step SSH configuration must be in place. Plus: ip scp server enable in global config to allow inbound SCP.

Cisco IOS SCP commands:

R1(config)# ip scp server enable
R1# copy scp: flash:      (pull file from remote SCP server to flash)
R1# copy flash: scp:      (push file from flash to remote SCP server)

SFTP vs SCP: Both use SSH (TCP 22). SFTP is a richer file-management protocol (list, rename, delete, chmod, resume). SCP is simpler — just copy. Cisco IOS supports SCP natively; SFTP support is less common on older IOS versions.

Security hierarchy summary:

  • TFTP: no auth, no encryption. Trusted networks only.
  • FTP: auth, no encryption. Cleartext credentials.
  • SCP: auth + encryption. The secure choice.

hack.memory: SCP = SSH + copy. Same port (22), same auth, same encryption. Most secure file transfer on Cisco = SCP. Prerequisite chain: 6-step SSH first, then 'ip scp server enable.' Security hierarchy cheat: TFTP < FTP < SCP (none < some < full).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer (or a real lab): complete SSH setup. Add 'ip scp server enable.' From a Linux client: 'scp myfile.txt admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/myfile.txt'. Verify with 'show flash:' on the router. Run Wireshark — all traffic on port 22 is encrypted ciphertext.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 and Day 43 together. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA exam tests: 'Most secure file transfer?' = SCP. 'Port?' = TCP 22. 'Prerequisite?' = SSH configured. Three one-word answers.

4.9.b.info:
FTP uses TCP port 21 as the control channel — the connection that carries login credentials, commands, and server responses. The control channel is opened by the client at the start of the session and stays open until the client sends QUIT or the session times out.

What flows on port 21:

  • Login: USER admin and PASS MyPassword — in plaintext.
  • Directory navigation: CWD /configs, PWD, LIST, NLST.
  • File management: DELE oldfile.cfg, RNFR / RNTO (rename), MKD / RMD (make/remove directory).
  • Transfer initiation: RETR filename (get) or STOR filename (put) — these trigger the data channel opening on port 20 (active) or a negotiated port (passive).
  • Session end: QUIT closes the control channel.

Server responses: The server replies on the same control channel with 3-digit status codes: 220 (service ready), 230 (login OK), 331 (user OK, need password), 530 (login failed), 550 (file not found), etc.

Cleartext risk on port 21: Because login and commands travel unencrypted, a packet sniffer on any intermediate segment sees the entire administrative session. This is why FTP is unacceptable across untrusted networks — use SFTP/SCP/FTPS instead.

Firewall rule for FTP: Always allow TCP 21 inbound for control. Data channel is negotiated separately — modern firewalls use FTP ALGs (Application Layer Gateways) to track the control channel and dynamically open the data channel.

hack.memory: Port 21 = the orders channel. Login, list, cd, get, put commands all flow here. Mnemonic: '21 gives orders.' Passwords are in cleartext — anyone sniffing TCP 21 reads everything.
hack.practice: Run Wireshark against a local FTP session. Filter on 'tcp.port == 21.' Follow the TCP stream. You will see USER, PASS, LIST, RETR commands in plain English. This is the single most visceral way to understand why FTP is not safe on untrusted networks.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA port-number recall question: 'FTP control port?' = TCP 21. Guaranteed on exam.

4.9.b.info:
FTP uses TCP port 20 as the data channel in active mode — the connection that carries actual file contents and directory listings. Unlike the control channel (which stays open), the data channel is opened fresh for each transfer and closed when the transfer completes.

Active mode sequence:

  1. Client opens control connection to server on TCP 21.
  2. Client sends PORT command telling the server which ephemeral port (e.g., 55123) the client will listen on.
  3. Server initiates the data connection from its port 20 to the client's announced port (55123).
  4. Data flows. When done, server closes the data connection.
  5. Control channel remains open for the next command.

Active mode firewall problem: The server calls back to the client on an ephemeral port. Client-side firewalls typically block unsolicited inbound connections — so active FTP fails through most firewalls. This is why passive mode was invented.

Passive mode sequence (contrast):

  1. Client opens control connection to server on TCP 21.
  2. Client sends PASV.
  3. Server replies with a high ephemeral port (e.g., 51234) that IT will listen on for data.
  4. Client opens the data connection from its ephemeral port to the server's 51234.
  5. Both connections are client-initiated — firewall-friendly.

Port 20 only applies in active mode. In passive mode, the data connection uses a negotiated server-side ephemeral port, not 20. On the exam, 'FTP data = TCP 20' refers specifically to active mode.

hack.memory: Port 20 = data in active mode only. Mnemonic: '20 does the work, 21 gives orders.' Active mode = server calls the client (firewall breaks). Passive mode = client calls the server (firewall OK). Modern clients default to passive.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer or a real lab: run an FTP transfer in active mode, then the same in passive. Watch the port numbers change. Active = server's port 20 to client ephemeral. Passive = client ephemeral to server ephemeral. This clarifies when port 20 actually appears on the wire.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA port-number recall: 'FTP data port?' = TCP 20 (active). Also: 'which mode is firewall-friendly?' = passive.

4.9.b.info:
FTP transmits usernames and passwords in cleartext on the TCP 21 control channel. Any attacker with access to any segment between the client and server can capture the USER and PASS commands verbatim. This is FTP's fundamental security failure and why it is being phased out of production environments.

What a capture looks like:

Client → Server (TCP 21): USER admin
Server → Client (TCP 21): 331 Password required for admin.
Client → Server (TCP 21): PASS MySecretP@ss
Server → Client (TCP 21): 230 User admin logged in.

The password MySecretP@ss is visible in the capture — no decoding, no decryption, just plain ASCII.

Secure alternatives:

  • SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol): Runs over SSH on TCP 22. Full file-management operations (list, rename, chmod). Credentials and data encrypted end-to-end.
  • SCP (Secure Copy Protocol): Also over SSH on TCP 22. Simpler — copy only. Cisco-preferred.
  • FTPS (FTP over TLS): FTP wrapped in TLS. Two flavors: implicit (TCP 990, TLS starts immediately) and explicit (TCP 21, AUTH TLS command negotiates TLS mid-session).

Exam mindset: If a question asks 'how to secure FTP credentials in transit,' the answer is one of: SFTP, SCP, or FTPS. If it asks 'which Cisco-native protocol is secure,' the answer is SCP.

Why this matters beyond the exam: Compliance frameworks (PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST) explicitly prohibit cleartext credential transmission. A production FTP deployment is usually a finding in any audit.

hack.memory: FTP = auth YES, encryption NO. Passwords in plaintext on TCP 21. Secure replacements: SFTP, SCP, FTPS. On Cisco, use SCP. Mnemonic: 'S is for Secure — SFTP, SCP, FTPS all start with S because they are Safer.'
hack.practice: Wireshark capture an FTP login session. Follow the TCP stream for port 21. Find the USER and PASS lines in plain English. Save that screenshot — it is the single most powerful visual for convincing anyone (including yourself) that plain FTP is unacceptable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA: 'Why is FTP insecure?' = cleartext credentials. 'Secure alternatives?' = SFTP, SCP, FTPS. 'Cisco-native secure?' = SCP.

4.9.c.info:
Cisco IOS uses a single unified copy command for all file transfer operations — between local locations (flash, NVRAM) and remote locations (TFTP, FTP, SCP servers). The syntax is always:

copy [source]: [destination]:

Critical rules:

  • Colons are required after each keyword. copy tftp flash is a syntax error. copy tftp: flash: is correct.
  • Source comes first, destination second. copy flash: tftp: copies FROM flash TO the TFTP server (backup). copy tftp: flash: copies FROM the TFTP server TO flash (upgrade).
  • Interactive prompts follow. After the command, IOS asks for the source filename, destination IP (if remote), and destination filename. Accept defaults with Enter when they are correct.

Common copy patterns:

copy running-config startup-config    (save config to NVRAM, same as 'write memory')
copy running-config tftp:             (backup active config to TFTP server)
copy tftp: running-config             (MERGE config from TFTP into active — additive)
copy tftp: startup-config             (REPLACE startup-config from TFTP)
copy tftp: flash:                     (download IOS image)
copy flash: tftp:                     (backup IOS image)
copy scp: flash:                      (secure IOS download over SSH)

The merge-vs-replace trap: copy tftp: running-config merges — new commands are added, existing commands are not removed. copy tftp: startup-config replaces — the entire startup-config is overwritten. This behavior difference is a guaranteed CCNA question.

Typical interactive flow:

R1# copy running-config tftp:
Address or name of remote host []? 192.168.1.100
Destination filename [r1-confg]? r1-backup-2026-04-16.cfg
!!
1523 bytes copied in 2.45 secs (621 bytes/sec)

nodes: Verify with show flash / show run

hack.memory: 'copy FROM TO' — always source first, destination second. Colons required. Mnemonic: 'copy FROM colon TO colon.' Exam trap: 'copy tftp: running-config' MERGES, 'copy tftp: startup-config' REPLACES.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: drill 5 copy commands in a row — 'copy run start,' 'copy run tftp:,' 'copy tftp: run,' 'copy tftp: start,' 'copy tftp: flash:.' After each, check the result with 'show flash:,' 'show run,' or 'show start.' Muscle-memory the syntax until you never forget the colons.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA tests the copy syntax in lab sims — missing colons, wrong order, or confusing merge/replace all lose points.

4.9.c.info:
The copy command accepts a specific vocabulary of source and destination keywords that map to protocols and local file system locations. Knowing this vocabulary cold lets you assemble any copy command on the exam.

Remote protocol keywords (followed by :):

  • tftp: — TFTP server (UDP 69).
  • ftp: — FTP server (TCP 20/21). Requires ip ftp username and ip ftp password.
  • scp: — SCP over SSH (TCP 22). Requires SSH configured and ip scp server enable.
  • http: / https: — HTTP(S) file server. Less common on routers.

Local file system keywords:

  • flash: — onboard flash storage. Primary location for IOS images.
  • nvram: — non-volatile RAM. Stores startup-config.
  • system: — virtual file system including running-config.
  • running-config — active config in RAM (shortcut for system:running-config).
  • startup-config — saved config in NVRAM (shortcut for nvram:startup-config).
  • usbflash0: / usbflash1: — USB drives on supported routers.
  • bootflash: — separate boot flash on some platforms.

Exam-favorite combinations:

copy running-config startup-config     (save config — most common)
copy tftp: flash:                       (IOS upgrade)
copy flash: tftp:                       (IOS backup)
copy running-config tftp:               (config backup)
copy tftp: startup-config               (config restore, REPLACE)

Filename tab-completion: IOS supports tab-completion inside copy. After typing copy flash:, press Tab to see available files. This is useful in lab sims where you do not remember the exact IOS filename.

hack.memory: Remote = 'protocol colon' (tftp:, ftp:, scp:). Local = 'device colon' (flash:, nvram:) OR 'shortcut' (running-config, startup-config). Memorize 5 remote + 5 local and you can build any copy command.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: practice every combo. 'copy run start,' 'copy run tftp:,' 'copy flash: tftp:,' 'copy tftp: flash:,' 'copy scp: flash:.' Verify each with a show command. Build the mental map of which keyword means which location.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA sims test the vocabulary — 'copy ___ ___' fill-in-the-blank style. Know both remote protocols and local locations.

4.9.d.info:
TFTP is the classic method for transferring Cisco IOS images to and from routers and switches. Despite being decades old, TFTP remains the default tool for IOS backups, upgrades, and recovery — its simplicity and near-zero resource overhead make it perfect for the job.

IOS upgrade workflow with TFTP:

  1. Stage the image on the TFTP server (e.g., copy c2900-universalk9-mz.SPA.157-3.M5.bin into the TFTP server's root directory).
  2. Verify flash space on the router: show flash:. New IOS images are often 50-200 MB — make sure free space exists. If not, delete older images with delete flash:oldimage.bin and squeeze flash:.
  3. Backup the current IOS: copy flash: tftp: — always do this before upgrading, in case the new image fails.
  4. Download the new image: copy tftp: flash:. Enter the TFTP server IP and the filename.
  5. Verify the image: show flash: confirms the file is present and its size matches what the vendor published. For strict integrity, check the MD5 hash with verify /md5 flash:newimage.bin.
  6. Configure boot system: boot system flash:c2900-universalk9-mz.SPA.157-3.M5.bin tells IOS which image to load on next reboot.
  7. Save config: write memory (or copy run start).
  8. Reload: reload. Router reboots into the new IOS.
  9. Verify post-reload: show version confirms the new IOS is running.

Why TFTP instead of FTP or SCP for IOS?

  • Simplicity — no auth config on the router. Just point and copy.
  • Speed on LAN — UDP has less overhead than TCP for trusted local transfers.
  • Every Cisco engineer has TFTP in their toolkit — it is the lowest-friction option.

When to use something else: Upgrading across untrusted links (use SCP), upgrading a large fleet with ACLs blocking UDP (use FTP or SCP), or when compliance demands authenticated transfers (SCP).

hack.memory: IOS upgrade = 6 steps: check flash, backup old, download new, verify MD5, boot system, reload. TFTP is the default because it is simple and fast on trusted LANs. Mnemonic: 'Backup, Transfer, Boot, Reload' = BTBR = 'Before Touching Boot, Reload.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: set up a TFTP server with a fake IOS image. On a router, practice the full upgrade workflow: show flash, copy flash: tftp:, copy tftp: flash:, boot system, write mem, reload. Verify with show version. Do it three times end-to-end until the sequence is automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA lab sim: IOS upgrade via TFTP. Know the copy command, boot system command, and the order of operations.

4.9.d.info:
SCP is the most secure file transfer protocol available on Cisco IOS. It is the exam answer to any question of the form 'which file transfer method should be used in a production environment?' or 'which protocol provides confidentiality and authentication for file transfers?'

Why SCP wins the security comparison:

  • Authentication: Uses SSH's user authentication — usernames in the local database (or AAA), passwords hashed with secret, optional public-key auth.
  • Encryption: Full SSH encryption (AES-128, AES-256, ChaCha20 depending on negotiation). Every byte of credential and data is ciphertext on the wire.
  • Integrity: HMAC-SHA1 or HMAC-SHA256 on every packet — any tampering is detected and the connection is dropped.
  • Replay protection: SSH sequence numbers prevent replay attacks.

SCP requirements on Cisco IOS:

  1. SSH fully configured (all 6 steps from topic 4.8).
  2. ip scp server enable in global config.
  3. A local user with sufficient privilege level (typically 15) to read/write flash.

Usage from a Linux/Mac client:

$ scp admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/config.txt ./backup.txt
admin@10.1.1.1's password:
config.txt  100%  1234  1.2KB/s  00:00

From a router (as SCP client):

R1# copy scp: flash:
Address or name of remote host []? 192.168.1.50
Source username [admin]? admin
Source filename []? newimage.bin
Destination filename [newimage.bin]?
Password:
!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!

Comparison summary:

  • TFTP: UDP 69. No auth. No crypto. Never secure.
  • FTP: TCP 20/21. Auth yes. Crypto no.
  • SCP: TCP 22. Auth yes. Crypto yes. Secure.

hack.memory: Most secure = SCP. Every time. TCP 22. Over SSH. Full encryption + auth. If the exam asks 'which is secure?' — answer SCP. If it asks 'which port?' — answer 22. If it asks 'prerequisite?' — answer SSH configured plus 'ip scp server enable.'
hack.practice: Set up a real SCP transfer: from your laptop to a Packet Tracer router (or real Cisco gear). Use 'scp myfile admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/.' Run Wireshark and confirm port 22 traffic is ciphertext. Compare to an FTP capture — night and day.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 and Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA guaranteed question: 'Most secure file transfer?' = SCP. Snap answer, no hesitation.

4.9.d.info:
The three file transfer protocols on the CCNA — TFTP, FTP, SCP — form a clear progression of features and security. Knowing this comparison table cold is worth multiple exam points.

The comparison table:

FeatureTFTPFTPSCP
TransportUDPTCPTCP
Port(s)6920 (data) + 21 (control)22
AuthenticationNoYes (cleartext)Yes (SSH)
EncryptionNoNoYes (SSH)
ReliabilityOwn ACK (UDP)TCPTCP
Use caseIOS on trusted LANLarge files, auth neededProduction, secure

Security ladder (memorize top-to-bottom):

  • TFTP — nothing (UDP 69, open).
  • FTP — auth only (TCP 20/21, cleartext creds).
  • SCP — auth + encryption (TCP 22, full SSH).

Variants to recognize on the exam:

  • SFTP (SSH FTP): richer file ops than SCP, same SSH transport (TCP 22).
  • FTPS (FTP over TLS): TCP 990 (implicit) or TCP 21 + AUTH TLS (explicit). Not on Cisco's core copy command set but commonly mentioned.

Typical exam framings: 'Which protocol is connectionless?' = TFTP. 'Which protocol uses two channels?' = FTP. 'Which encrypts everything?' = SCP. 'Which is most secure?' = SCP. 'Which is least secure?' = TFTP.

layers: TFTP: UDP 69 — no auth, no crypto

hack.memory: Ladder from least to most secure: TFTP → FTP → SCP. Ports: 69 → 20/21 → 22. Auth: no → yes → yes. Crypto: no → no → yes. Write this table on your whiteboard at exam start. Three protocols, six facts, infinite exam points.
hack.practice: Make a physical flashcard with the comparison table. Drill until you can fill in every cell from memory in under 30 seconds. Then drill the reverse: given a feature (e.g., 'UDP'), name the protocol (TFTP). This two-way drill cements it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA tests this comparison in every form — multiple choice, fill-in-the-blank, 'match the feature to the protocol.' Master the table and you own this topic.

4.9.e.info:
copy tftp: running-config (or copy scp:/ftp: running-config) merges the incoming config file with the currently active running-config. It does NOT replace or wipe existing commands. New commands are added. Commands already present are left alone. Commands that exist in running but are absent from the incoming file are NOT removed.

Concrete example:

Current running-config:                Incoming config file (from TFTP):
hostname R1                            hostname R1
interface Gi0/0                        interface Gi0/1
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0      ip address 10.2.2.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown                            no shutdown
no ip domain-lookup                    router ospf 1
                                        network 10.2.2.0 0.0.0.255 area 0

Result after copy tftp: running-config:

hostname R1
interface Gi0/0
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown
interface Gi0/1                           ← ADDED
 ip address 10.2.2.1 255.255.255.0         ← ADDED
 no shutdown                               ← ADDED
no ip domain-lookup                         ← KEPT
router ospf 1                             ← ADDED
 network 10.2.2.0 0.0.0.255 area 0         ← ADDED

Why merge instead of replace? Running-config is LIVE — the device is actively passing traffic. A wholesale replace would require tearing down every interface, stopping every protocol, and rebuilding — disruptive and dangerous. Merging lets you layer in changes without killing traffic.

The gotcha: If the incoming config has contradictory commands (e.g., different IP on the same interface), the incoming command overrides — but commands not mentioned at all are left as they were. This means you cannot 'clean' a config via merge. To get a truly fresh state, use copy tftp: startup-config + reload.

hack.memory: 'copy tftp: running-config' = MERGE (additive). Think 'paste on top, nothing erased.' If you need to wipe, use 'copy tftp: startup-config' + reload, NOT merge. Mnemonic: 'Running is Running — too busy to Replace.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure running-config with 3 unique commands. Prepare a TFTP file with 3 DIFFERENT commands. Run 'copy tftp: running-config' and compare. You will see all 6 commands present. This makes merge behavior unforgettable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA merge-vs-replace trap is a near-guaranteed question. Know: running = MERGE, startup = REPLACE.

4.9.e.info:
copy tftp: startup-config (or from SCP/FTP) replaces the entire startup-config in NVRAM with the incoming file. Every command in the old startup-config is wiped. The new config takes effect only after the next reload — the running-config in RAM is unaffected until then.

Why replace (not merge)? Startup-config is STATIC — it is not actively driving traffic. Replacing it is safe because nothing is running against it. The new config only becomes active after reload, giving the admin a clean rebuild from the new config.

Standard workflow for a full config refresh:

  1. copy running-config tftp: — backup the current running-config, in case you need to roll back.
  2. copy tftp: startup-config — pull the new config into NVRAM, replacing the old startup.
  3. Verify with show startup-config — confirm the new file is in place.
  4. reload — device reboots, loads new startup-config as the running-config.
  5. Verify post-reload with show running-config — should match what was pulled from TFTP.

DO NOT confuse with running-config behavior:

  • copy tftp: running-config — MERGE (additive, immediate, disruption depends on deltas).
  • copy tftp: startup-config — REPLACE (wipes startup, takes effect at reload).

When to pick which: Use MERGE (running-config) for incremental changes on a live device. Use REPLACE (startup-config + reload) for major rebuilds, RMA (return-materials-authorization), swapouts, or when you want a guaranteed clean state.

Classic exam trap: A question shows a config copied to startup-config and asks 'why does traffic still follow the old config?' Answer: the device has not been reloaded yet. Running-config in RAM is still the old version.

hack.memory: 'copy tftp: startup-config' = REPLACE (wholesale). Effect at RELOAD, not immediately. Running-config still the old one until reboot. Mnemonic: 'Startup is Sleeping — safe to Swap.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: backup the running-config to TFTP, modify running-config with 3 new commands, then 'copy tftp: startup-config' (using the old backup). Check 'show running-config' — still has 3 new commands. Reload. Check again — 3 new commands gone, startup-config has taken over. This makes the delayed effect obvious.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA favorite question: 'You copied config to startup-config but nothing changed — why?' = need to reload. Also: 'which copy wipes existing config?' = startup-config.

4.9.e.info:
show flash: lists the contents of the router's flash storage — the location where IOS images, backup configs, and occasionally other files are kept. It is the go-to command for verifying IOS image presence before and after an upgrade.

Typical output:

R1# show flash:
-#- --length-- -----date/time------ path
1   104623616  Apr 16 2026 10:20:45  c2900-universalk9-mz.SPA.157-3.M5.bin
2   2181       Apr 15 2026 14:03:12  config.text.backup
3   3904       Apr 14 2026 09:11:28  vlan.dat

  262144000 bytes available (104627800 bytes used)

Fields to understand:

  • Length: file size in bytes. IOS images are typically 50-200 MB.
  • Date/time: when the file was written. Useful for identifying recent changes.
  • Path: filename. IOS image filenames encode model, feature set, and version (e.g., c2900-universalk9-mz.SPA.157-3.M5.bin).
  • Bytes available: free flash space. Check this before downloading a new IOS.

Pre-upgrade check: Always run show flash: before copy tftp: flash:. If free space is less than the new IOS size, the copy will fail mid-transfer. Make room by deleting old images: delete flash:oldimage.bin, then squeeze flash: on some older platforms to reclaim space.

Post-upgrade verification: After copy tftp: flash:, rerun show flash: to confirm the new file is present and the size matches the vendor-published size. For strict integrity, compute the MD5: verify /md5 flash:newimage.bin [expected-md5].

Related commands:

  • dir flash: — similar output, often used interchangeably.
  • show file systems — lists all file systems (flash, nvram, system, usbflash0, etc.) and their sizes.
  • show boot — shows which IOS image the router is configured to boot next.

hack.memory: 'show flash:' = what is in flash right now. Use it BEFORE copying (check space) and AFTER (verify image landed). Mnemonic: 'Show flash before and after — like a before/after photo for IOS.' Always verify size matches vendor.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: run 'show flash:' on a fresh router. Note the IOS file and free space. Copy a dummy file via TFTP. Rerun 'show flash:' — see the new file and reduced free space. Delete it with 'delete flash:dummy.' Rerun — file gone, space reclaimed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA tests 'show flash:' output interpretation — identifying the IOS file, reading free space, spotting the current image.

4.9.f.info:
SCP (Secure Copy Protocol) is the Cisco-preferred secure file transfer mechanism. It rides entirely on top of SSH — same TCP port (22), same authentication mechanism (local users or AAA), same encryption (AES-128/256, ChaCha20 depending on negotiation), same integrity (HMAC-SHA1/256). There is no separate SCP daemon or separate port.

Why SCP instead of FTP or TFTP?

  • Confidentiality: Every byte encrypted. No credential or data leakage.
  • Authentication: Reuses SSH's strong auth — username/secret or public-key.
  • Integrity: SSH's MAC detects tampering.
  • Single port: TCP 22, which is already open for SSH admin access. No extra firewall rules.

Cisco IOS configuration to enable SCP server:

R1(config)# ip scp server enable

That single command, combined with a working SSH setup, turns the router into an SCP target — any authorized SSH user can now push or pull files.

Usage from an external client (Linux/Mac/Windows with OpenSSH):

$ scp admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/backup.cfg ./local-backup.cfg
admin@10.1.1.1's password:
backup.cfg  100%  1234  12.3KB/s  00:00

Usage from the router as an SCP client:

R1# copy scp: flash:
Address or name of remote host []? 192.168.1.50
Source username [admin]? admin
Source filename []? newimage.bin
Destination filename [newimage.bin]?
Password:
!!!!!!!!!!

Privilege level matters: The SSH user needs privilege level 15 (or sufficient role-based access) to read/write flash. Level 1 users get 'permission denied.'

hack.memory: SCP = SSH + file copy. Port 22. Prerequisite: working SSH + 'ip scp server enable.' User needs privilege 15. Mnemonic: 'SCP is SSH's little brother — inherits everything, adds file transfer.'
hack.practice: From a Linux laptop: 'scp admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/running-config ./backup.cfg.' Capture with Wireshark — verify TCP 22 traffic is ciphertext. From router: 'copy scp: flash:' and pull a file from your laptop (running a simple SSH server). Two-way SCP mastered.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 and Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA: 'Most secure file transfer?' = SCP. 'Port?' = 22. 'Requires?' = SSH configured + ip scp server enable.

4.9.f.info:
SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) and SCP (Secure Copy Protocol) both run over SSH on TCP 22 and provide identical security guarantees. The difference is the richness of file operations each supports.

SCP (Secure Copy):

  • Simple copy only — get file, put file.
  • No directory listing, no renaming, no deletion, no resume.
  • Lightweight — fewer messages on the wire.
  • Cisco IOS supports SCP natively (and has for a long time).

SFTP (SSH File Transfer):

  • Full file-management protocol: list, rename, delete, chmod, chown, mkdir, rmdir, resume interrupted transfers, query file attributes.
  • Modeled after a filesystem API over SSH.
  • Clients like FileZilla, WinSCP, and sftp CLI treat the remote filesystem like a mounted share.
  • Cisco IOS SFTP support is limited and version-dependent. SCP is the Cisco default.

Protocol positioning (all use TCP 22 / SSH):

  • SCP — the 'rcp over SSH' replacement. Simple, fast.
  • SFTP — the 'FTP over SSH' replacement. Rich file operations.

Easy confusion to avoid: SFTP is not FTPS. SFTP uses SSH (port 22). FTPS uses FTP + TLS (ports 990 or 21). Different protocols, different history. Exam may probe this distinction.

When to use which on Cisco: Almost always SCP, because it is what IOS supports best. SFTP is available on some newer platforms but not universally.

hack.memory: Both over SSH, both TCP 22. SCP = simple copy. SFTP = full file management. On Cisco: SCP is default. SFTP = SSH-based, NOT the same as FTPS (which is FTP + TLS). Mnemonic: 'SCP for Cisco, SFTP for servers.'
hack.practice: On a Linux box with OpenSSH server: use both 'scp file host:/path' and 'sftp host' (interactive). In sftp, try 'ls,' 'put,' 'get,' 'rename,' 'rm.' SCP cannot do most of these. This lab clarifies the feature gap.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 mentions SFTP briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA tests SCP primarily; SFTP appears as a comparison option. Know that both run over SSH on TCP 22 and both are secure.

4.9.f.info:
ip scp server enable is the single global-config command that enables Cisco IOS to act as an SCP server, allowing external clients to push or pull files via SCP.

Exact command:

R1(config)# ip scp server enable

Prerequisites (must be in place first):

  1. Full SSH configuration (the 6-step recipe from topic 4.8).
  2. At least one local user with privilege level 15 (or AAA configured for elevated access).
  3. VTY lines configured for SSH transport and login local.

What it does: Before this command, SCP attempts against the router fail with 'connection refused for SCP' even if SSH works for interactive login. After this command, the router listens for SCP subsystem requests inside SSH sessions and responds appropriately.

Permission behavior:

  • The SSH user's privilege level determines what they can do via SCP.
  • Privilege 15 = full read/write across the file system (flash, nvram, system).
  • Lower privilege = permission denied for most file operations.

Verification:

  • show running-config | include scp — confirms the command is in the config.
  • Test from a client: scp admin@10.1.1.1:flash:/running-config ./backup. If the file transfers, SCP is working.

Why disabled by default: Security. SCP allows authenticated users to read or write the device's file system remotely — including overwriting IOS images. Requiring an explicit opt-in prevents accidental exposure.

To disable: no ip scp server enable. SCP attempts will resume being refused while SSH interactive login continues to work.

hack.memory: 'ip scp server enable' = one-liner to turn on SCP on Cisco. Prerequisite: SSH working + local user priv 15. Default: DISABLED (security opt-in). Mnemonic: 'SSH builds the tunnel, ip scp server enable opens the file-copy door.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: complete SSH setup. Try SCP from a Linux client — fails (SCP not enabled). Add 'ip scp server enable.' Retry — success. Remove with 'no ip scp server enable.' Retry — fails again. Clear cause-and-effect.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 and Day 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. CCNA command-recall: 'Which command enables SCP server on Cisco?' = 'ip scp server enable.' Low weight but exactly worded on some exams.
